Oil Edges Higher; Rising Stockpiles, Trade Uncertainty to Weigh — Market Talk
data.description: Oil edges higher in early Asian trade, after falling overnight as data showed a weekly climb of almost 9 million barrels in U.S. crude inventories. Crude oil prices may continue to be weighed by rising U.S. stockpiles and trade-related uncertainty, says Tickmill's Joseph Dahrieh in a note. This reinforces a bearish sentiment due to persistent demand-side risks, the managing principal writes. Although supply constraints would provide some stability, expectations of rising production in the U.S. and from OPEC members could weigh on the market over the longer-term, he says. Front-month WTI crude oil futures rise 0.2% to $71.20/bbl; front-month Brent crude oil futures are up 0.1% to $74.71/bbl.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

data.title: Indonesia's Steady Growth Momentum May Keep Central Bank in Wait-and-See Mode — Market Talk
data.description: Indonesia's 1Q GDP growth is expected to remain steady, supported by increased government social spending that could boost household consumption, CIMB economists say in a note. The economists keep Indonesia's 2025 GDP growth estimate at 5.0%. With supportive growth and external uncertainties, Bank Indonesia is likely to take a wait-and-see approach, balancing stability and growth while strategically reducing the policy rate only when a favorable window arises, they say. CIMB maintains its forecast for one 25bps rate cut per quarter, bringing the policy rate to 5.00% by end-2025.(yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

Most JGBs Edge Higher, Tracking Price Gains in U.S. Treasurys — Market Talk
data.description: Most JGBs edge higher in the morning Tokyo session, tracking price gains in U.S. Treasurys overnight. Both JGBs and Treasurys tend to move in tandem. Today's auction of 900 billion yen in 30-year sovereign debt by Japan's Finance Ministry is likely to be digested smoothly and the 30-year yield is expected to hover around 2.3%, Citi Research's Tomohisa Fujiki says in a research report. Given the Bank of Japan's schedule of JGB purchase operations, investors may also prefer yield-curve flatteners, the rates strategist adds. The JGB 30-year yield is down 1 bps at 2.315%; 10-year yield is down 0.5 bps at 1.275%. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: ChiNext Price Index Opens 0.2% Lower at 2059.45
REA Reports 'Very Strong' Fiscal First-Half Results, Details on Costs Guidance Downgrade Required, Jarden Research Says
data.description:
REA Group reported "very strong" fiscal first-half results, and downgraded its fiscal year cost guidance, now expecting low double-digit core operating cost growth, from the previous high single-digit forecast, according to a Thursday note by Jarden Research,

The digital advertising company reported Thursday that group revenue for the six months to Dec. 31, 2024, was AU$872.9 million, up 20% from AU$725.5 million a year earlier, beating Jarden's AU$869 million guidance.

Group earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization before associates was AU$535 million, up 22% from last year, and 1.7% above the market's expectations of AU$527 million, Jarden noted.

Jarden estimates a AU$12 million to AU$20 million increase to REA's fiscal 2025 cost outlook, with first-half costs already AU$10m above consensus, implying an AU$2 million to AU$10m rise in costs for the fiscal second half.

Further clarification is needed on whether the cost downgrade is due to higher revenue expectations or a changed revenue mix, Jarden said.

Jarden Research has an underweight rating on REA Group with an AU$198 price target.

data.title: South Korea's Inflation Picks Up in January
data.description:
South Korea's inflation rate rose 2.2% in January from a year earlier, marking the fastest on-year growth in five months, according to Statistics Korea on Thursday.

January's figure was the highest inflation rate since July last year when the index reached 2.6%.

The index increased 0.7% from December 2024.

The increase was due to an uptick in the prices of food and non-alcoholic beverages, housing, water, electricity, gas and other fuels, furnishings, household equipment and routine maintenance, health, transport, recreation and culture, education, restaurants and hotels, and miscellaneous goods and services.

The core consumer price index, which excludes food and energy prices, rose 0.5% in January from a month prior and increased 1.9% from a year ago.

data.title: Native Mineral Resources Plans Queensland Gold Project Drilling Program; Shares Reach Near Two-Year High
data.description:
Native Mineral Resources Holdings is planning an 18-hole diamond drilling program at the Blackjack gold project in Queensland, according to a Thursday Australian bourse filing.

It plans to restart production at the project in the third quarter of 2025.

The firm brought in MEC Mining to review historical drilling at the project. The review indicated the presence of anomalous mineralization within 50 meters of the surface of the project.

Native Mineral's shares rose almost 8% in recent trading on Thursday and earlier hit their highest since March 2023.

data.title: Hang Seng Index Opens Flat at 20598.98
Hang Seng Index Tech Opens 0.1% Lower at 4926.54
Philippines Jobless Rate Steady at 3.1%
data.description:
The unemployment rate in the Philippines stood at 3.1% in December 2024, unchanged from the same month last year.

This marked the lowest jobless rate since June, with the number of unemployed individuals slightly increasing to 1.63 million from 1.60 million in December 2023, while employment fell to 50.19 million from 50.52 million.

Among broad industry groups, the services sector continued as the top sector in terms of the number of employed persons with a share of 60.5% percent of the total number of employed.

Meanwhile, the labor force participation rate declined to 65.1%, compared to 66.6% a year earlier.

On average, employed persons worked 40.9 hours per week, lower than the 41.1 hours recorded in December 2023.

data.title: MicroStrategy raises another $600 million from preferred stock sales to buy bitcoin
data.description:
By Tomi Kilgore and Claudia Assis

MicroStrategy, which has changed its name, has spent nearly half of a planned $42 billion raise to buy bitcoin since the end of October

Strategy Inc., formerly known as MicroStrategy Inc., said late Wednesday it has raised an additional $584 million through the sale of preferred stock, and it confirmed it is about halfway through its three-year plan of buying $42 billion in the cryptocurrency.

Strategy's (MSTR) $20.5 billion worth of bitcoin bought since the end of the third quarter was its "largest ever" bump in quarterly holdings of the cryptocurrency, the company said.

"We carried this strong momentum forward" into the first quarter, Chief Financial Officer Andrew Kang said, raising the additional $584 million offering, which was supported by both institutional and retail investors.

The stock slipped 1.4% in Wednesday's after-hours session, after closing the regular session down 3.3% at $336.70.

The company, which announced its name change earlier Wednesday, is for most investors a bitcoin play.

In its last earnings report, MicroStrategy stunned investors by announcing a plan to raise $42 billion over the next three years, through the sale of common stock and debt, so that it could buy bitcoin (BTCUSD).

In just three months, the company has already completed nearly half of its three-year plan, as it spent $20.5 billion to buy 218,887 bitcoins from Oct. 31 through Jan. 26.

Read: MicroStrategy is still loading up on bitcoin, but the pace of buying is slowing.

As Mizuho analyst Dan Dolev wrote in a recent note to clients, while bitcoin itself has no intrinsic value, increasing global adoption of the digital currency, a slow rate of bitcoin supply growth and a favorable political environment suggest bitcoin prices will continue to rise.

Also read: Trump vows to build U.S. bitcoin reserve. Crypto community is cheering.

And MicroStrategy's stock needs a boost. While the stock closed Wednesday about 36% above where it closed before the company's last earnings report, it has stalled in the last couple months, and has lost about 29% below its postearnings, postelection record close of $473.83 on Nov. 20.

Meanwhile, bitcoin didn't peak until Dec. 17, when it hit an all-time high of $109,224, and was recently trading about 11% below that peak.

In the company's last disclosure, it said it had spent a total of about $30.4 billion to buy 471,107 bitcoins, at an average price of $64,511 per bitcoin. At current prices, that holding would be worth roughly $45.6 billion.

As for MicroStrategy's software business, which Mizuho's Dolev said was a nearly "inconsequential portion" of its valuation, the company reported revenue of $120.7 million, a 3% drop year-over-year, including subscription services revenues of $31.9 million, a 48.4% jump year-over-year.

Analysts surveyed by FactSet expected revenue of $122.4 million.

The company also reported a net loss was $670.8 million, or $3.03 a share, swinging from earnings of 50 cents a share in the fourth quarter of 2023. FactSet consensus called for a loss of 9 cents a share.

-Tomi Kilgore -Claudia Assis

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Terra Uranium unveils co-ordinated exploration strategy for Athabasca Basin
data.description: Terra Uranium has formulated a co-ordinated exploration strategy to advance its project suite in Canada’s highly prospective Athabasca Basin to drill-ready status. The strategy will focus on nine high-quality assets covering 1,817 square kilometres of the Tier 1 district, where maiden diamond drilling has confirmed the presence of uranium. The company has contracted field work for the new strategy to Axiom Exploration Services, which Terra first appointed in November to scout new infrastructure and claim access for the upcoming drilling program. Drill-ready targets Terra signed an option agreement with ATHA Energy in August to acquire up to 60% of its Pasfield Lake development near Saskatchewan. In return, ATHA agreed to grant Terra an option to acquire up to 70% of its Spire and Horizon projects in the same region. Terra has 18 drill-ready targets across its HawkRock, Pasfield and Parker projects that will be farmed out for deeper drilling to larger companies including ATHA. Geophysical work will commence this year at HawkRock and Pasfield East, while the base camp at Pasfield Lake will be maintained, ready for drilling by third-party partners. ATHA has the option to begin drilling at Pasfield Lake in 2025 in concert with exploration of its Ridge project, situated directly to the south along the underexplored Cable Bay Shear Zone, and access to the Pasfield Lake base camp. Spire and Horizon plans Terra completed maiden exploration at the Spire and Horizon projects in late 2024 under the ATHA option agreement. The company’s evaluation of airborne geophysics from that campaign will allow it to plan a program to test shallower uranium targets during 2025. Previous boulder train sampling at Horizon included results of up to 6,034 parts per million uranium (0.71% uranium oxide) and 30,000ppm uranium (3.53% uranium oxide), with an up-ice direction on the project area. Strategy roll-out Terra executive chair Andrew Vigar said the company was keen to roll out the new strategy. “We will focus on testing the uranium targets developed over the last three years and we look to expand our partnering approach with groups such as ATHA Energy for drilling,” he said. “Our asset portfolio now totals an impressive nine projects and one pending acquisition in the majority of the Athabasca Basin and we look forward to taking Terra to the next level with a discovery in 2025.”

data.title: Indonesia GDP Growth May Improve This Year — Market Talk
data.description: Indonesia's economic growth is expected to improve in 2025, driven by increased government spending that should strengthen domestic demand, UOB economists Enrico Tanuwidjaja and Shaun Wirawan Susanto say in a note. Investment spending may accelerate in the coming quarters, particularly in sectors like agriculture, mining and processed metals, they reckon. Higher fiscal and investment spending could boost confidence and support household consumption, they add. UOB maintains its 2025 GDP growth forecast for the country at 5.2%. Indonesia's GDP growth was 5.03% in 2024. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

data.title: Warriedar Resources Reports Gold Mineralization at Western Australia Project; Shares Fall 2%
data.description:
Warriedar Resources said assay results from scout drilling its Golden Range project in Western Australia intercepted gold mineralization, according to a Thursday Australian bourse filing.

The intercepts include ones that reported 10 meters at 2 grams-per-tonne (g/t) grade of gold from a depth of 124 meters as well as two meters at 3.9 g/t grade of gold from a depth of 78 meters.

It conducted a 17-hole reverse circulation drilling program for a total depth of 3,155 meters at the project in the fourth quarter of 2024.

The firm plans to conduct growth-focused drilling at the Windinne Well deposit in the project in February.

The firm's shares fell past 2% in recent trading on Thursday.

data.title: Costco starts off 2025 with yet another sales gain
data.description:
By Bill Peters

Same-store sales rose 7.5% last month

Costco Wholesale Corp. started the year off with yet another sales gain, saying in a release that revenue last month rose 9.2% year over year to $19.51 billion.

For the four weeks ending Feb. 2, same-store sales rose 7.5%, Costco (COST) said on Wednesday. Sales in the smaller e-commerce segment rose 13.6%.

Traffic was up during the month, and higher gas prices helped nudge sales higher, according to a recorded message discussing the results. A tougher foreign-exchange backdrop acted as a counterweight, however.

Costco shares were up nearly 0.2% after hours on Wednesday. As of the close of trading Wednesday, the stock was up 44.9% over the past 12 months.

The sales increase for January follows a slightly higher gain in December, when holiday-season demand and a jump in online sales helped results. More shoppers, stretched by higher costs of living, have turned to the biggest retailers - like Costco, Walmart Inc. (WMT) and Amazon.com Inc. (AMZN) - for bargains and essentials.

Those mass retailers, in turn, have started to compete more aggressively with smaller ones in areas like drug prescriptions and beauty products. Costco has also said it sees opportunities to attract wealthier shoppers looking to save money.

-Bill Peters

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Ford's weak guidance sends automaker's stock sliding
data.description:
By Claudia Assis

Ford 'relentlessly focused on cost,' incoming CFO says

Ford Motor Co. shares dropped 4.5% in after-hours trading Wednesday after the automaker hit a quarterly sales record but raised alarm with a light guidance for the year, saying profits will be marred by "headwinds related to market factors."

Ford (F) called for 2025 adjusted profit before interest and taxes of between $7 billion and $8.5 billion, which would be down from last year's adjusted earnings before interest and taxes of $10.2 billion. It would also be weaker than Wall Street's consensus for $8.3 billion.

The weak guidance was not entirely a surprise, but it was "considerably worse than expected," CFRA analyst Garrett Nelson said.

Wednesday's print is likely to put more pressure on Ford's management, as investors may be losing patience with "Ford's poor results and lackluster shareholder returns," he said.

On a conference call with analysts after the earnings release, Chief Executive Jim Farley touched upon the Trump administration's tariff plan, which wreaked havoc on markets and especially impacted automaker stocks earlier this week before the planned tariffs on Canada and Mexico were put on ice.

There's no question that the 25% planned tariffs would have a "huge impact on our industry, with billions of dollars of industry profits wiped out" and negative effects on U.S. jobs as well as the "entire value system in our industry," Farley said.

Ford would withstand Canada and Mexico tariffs lasting a few weeks, making use of its inventories, he said. Longer term, however, there would be a need for "major strategy shifts" and the impact would be devastating.

Farley also called for a more "comprehensive" tariff policy, because the current plans are a "bonanza" to import competitors such as Toyota (TM).

"Tariffs would also mean higher prices for customers," he said. However, Ford has had conversations with the administration and with lawmakers, and executives believe they are committed to strengthening the auto industry, he said.

Ford also called for a breakeven current quarter, thanks to 20% lower wholesale revenue and an "unfavorable mix" that includes ongoing expenses with some of its major U.S. assembly plants.

Making matters worse for Ford, its U.S. dealers' inventories increased to 103 days, from 81 days, Tom Narayan at RBC Capital said.

"While this was likely due in part to seasonality, it does raise questions on how high Ford can keep pricing," and Ford is indeed calling for pricing headwinds and lower volumes, Narayan said.

Ford's vice president of finance and incoming Chief Financial Officer Sherry House said that the company is "relentlessly focused on cost."

House will takes the reins from longtime CFO John Lawler on Thursday, a move that was announced in May. Lawler, a Ford veteran of more than 30 years and CFO since October 2020, will become Ford's vice chair, focusing on strategy and corporate development.

Ford also called for a $5 billion to $5.5 billion annual loss on its EV unit, Ford E, after the unit lost $5.1 billion last year. House said that the company was able to increase volumes and continues to invest in its next-generation electric vehicles.

Ford's sales for the fourth quarter clocked in at $48.3 billion, a 2% increase over the fourth quarter of 2023. The automaker earned an adjusted 39 cents a share in the quarter, up from 29 cents a share in the year-ago period.

Analysts polled by FactSet expected adjusted EPS of 34 cents a share on sales of $47.4 billion

Ford's EV unit narrowed its losses in the fourth quarter to $1.39 billion, from $1.57 billion a year ago.

Shares of Ford have lost 22% in the past 12 months, contrasting with gains of around 22% for the S&P 500 index SPX.

-Claudia Assis

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Australia's Goods Surplus Narrows in December 2024
data.description:
Australia's goods surplus narrowed by AU$1.71 billion to AU$5.09 billion between November and December 2024, the Australian Bureau of Statistics reported Thursday.

Goods exports rose 1.1% to AU$44.03 billion, while goods imports rose 5.9% to AU$38.94 billion.

Non-rural goods drove exports as they rose 2.3% or AU$772 million. This was due to higher exports of metal ores and minerals.

Meanwhile, imported capital goods went up nearly 11% or AU$917 million, while imported consumption goods rose 3.6% or AU$423 million.
